refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) in the teleoperation link to tolerate packet loss and maintain command/feedback fidelity.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication paths (dual network interfaces, carrier diversity) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-C: Use a hybrid approach combining protocol-level retransmission (TCP-like), FEC, and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization for critical telemetry and tasking streams.
DP-D: Implement a lightweight, deterministic teleoperation protocol with built-in packet loss tolerance and operator-side command buffering to mask transient network degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Use a hybrid approach combining protocol-level retransmission (TCP-like), FEC, and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization for critical telemetry and tasking streams.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation packet loss mitigation to network infrastructure selection (DP for FR on network provisioning), but this is a necessary and acceptable dependency.
DP-C couples to safety alert prioritization (SafetyAlert interface must be prioritized over non-critical telemetry), which is a logical and required coupling.
DP-C does not create circular dependencies or hidden functional requirements.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred over DP-A (FEC alone is insufficient for high-loss scenarios), DP-B (redundancy adds cost and complexity without addressing protocol-level loss), and DP-D (deterministic protocols may not adapt to varying network conditions). DP-C balances robustness, adaptability, and cost.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable during operation. Risk: If conditions vary significantly, the system may not meet the packet loss target.
Assumption: Retransmission overhead (latency increase) is acceptable for non-critical telemetry. Risk: If latency becomes excessive, operator control responsiveness may degrade.
Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is within the available network capacity. Risk: If bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may not be deployable.
Assumption: QoS prioritization is supported by the underlying network infrastructure. Risk: If the network does not support QoS, prioritization will fail.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define: (1) which telemetry/tasking streams are prioritized (e.g., SafetyAlert > Tasking > Telemetry), (2) FEC redundancy level (e.g., 20% overhead), (3) retransmission timeout and retry limits, and (4) fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds the target despite mitigation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol implementation, FEC/retransmission logic, QoS prioritization, and packet loss monitoring. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert). It depends on the Network Link interface for physical/logical connectivity.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) flow from operator console into Teleoperation module. Tasking stream is prioritized for retransmission and FEC.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, operational status) flows from Vehicle module through Teleoperation module to operator console. Telemetry is subject to adaptive quality-of-service based on available bandwidth and packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts (emergency stop, geofence breach, stability warnings) flow bidirectionally between Safety Services and Teleoperation module. SafetyAlert is highest priority for FEC and retransmission.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network connectivity (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) provided by underlying network infrastructure. Teleoperation module monitors Network Link quality and adapts FEC/QoS accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural and primary owner of packet loss mitigation logic. All protocol-level FEC, retransmission, and QoS prioritization are contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only dependency is on Network Link interface for physical connectivity.

Vehicle module should not own teleoperation packet loss mitigation; it is a consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry. Assigning DP-C to Vehicle would scatter protocol logic across multiple modules and violate separation of concerns.

Safety Services owns safety alert generation and validation, not communication protocol implementation. While SafetyAlert prioritization is part of DP-C, the core FEC/retransmission logic belongs in Teleoperation. Safety Services interfaces with Teleoperation via SafetyAlert.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-C to NGHE Solution would not provide architectural clarity or ownership. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module can fully contain the hybrid FEC/retransmission/QoS approach (DP-C) with clear ownership of protocol logic, packet loss monitoring, and adaptive quality-of-service. External dependencies are limited to Network Link (physical connectivity) and interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert).
containment_risks: Risk: If network conditions change rapidly (e.g., WiFi to cellular handoff), the Teleoperation module may not adapt QoS fast enough, causing temporary packet loss spikes. Mitigation: Implement rapid network condition detection and QoS adjustment (sub-second timescale).

Risk: If the Vehicle module or Safety Services module introduce their own retransmission logic, packet loss mitigation will be duplicated and potentially conflicting. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contracts and ownership boundaries; document that Teleoperation is the sole owner of end-to-end packet loss mitigation.

Risk: If the Network Link interface does not provide sufficient bandwidth or QoS support, the Teleoperation module cannot achieve the target packet loss limit. Mitigation: Validate network capacity and QoS capabilities during system integration; escalate to network provisioning if insufficient.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly document the Teleoperation module's responsibilities: (1) implement FEC encoder/decoder, (2) manage retransmission queue and timeout logic, (3) monitor packet loss metrics in real-time, (4) adapt QoS prioritization based on network conditions, and (5) expose packet loss metrics via a monitoring interface to Safety Services and operator console. Define clear interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link to prevent diffusion of packet loss logic into other modules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid FEC/retransmission/QoS) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The DP is specific, implementable, and measurable. No functional gaps or misalignments detected. The DP assumes customer-agreed network conditions are stable and that QoS is supported by the network infrastructure; these assumptions are documented and acceptable.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-C. No scattering of protocol logic across Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution.
Interface clarity: Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link are well-defined and owned by appropriate modules. No ambiguity in responsibility.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-C. The 5% diffusion is acceptable and limited to dependency on Network Link interface (external to Teleoperation). All protocol-level logic is contained within Teleoperation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics via monitoring interface. Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions) are measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear and unambiguous. Ownership, interfaces, and dependencies are well-defined. No allocation conflicts with other FRs or modules.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify FEC redundancy level, retransmission timeout, and QoS prioritization rules (e.g., SafetyAlert > Tasking > Telemetry).
Document Teleoperation module responsibilities and interface contracts to prevent diffusion of packet loss logic.
Define fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds the target despite mitigation (e.g., operator alert, graceful degradation, emergency stop).
Establish acceptance criteria for network conditions (e.g., latency, jitter, available bandwidth) to validate DP-C feasibility.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been decomposed into three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, protocol selection/configuration, and monitoring/reporting). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection to ensure complete coverage of packet loss mitigation. The current level (FR-NGHE-009 → DP-C → Teleoperation) is sound and passes all gates, but the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to achieve implementable, testable requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined, documented, and stable during operation.
QoS prioritization is supported by the underlying network infrastructure (WiFi, cellular, or hybrid).
Retransmission overhead (latency increase) is acceptable for non-critical telemetry streams.
FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is within available network capacity.
Operator console and Vehicle module can tolerate the latency introduced by FEC/retransmission logic.
Safety Services module will provide SafetyAlert stream with clear priority indicators.
open_risks: Network conditions may vary significantly during operation, causing packet loss to exceed the target threshold despite mitigation.
Rapid network handoffs (WiFi to cellular) may cause transient packet loss spikes if QoS adaptation is not fast enough.
If network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC overhead may not be deployable, limiting packet loss mitigation effectiveness.
Operator control responsiveness may degrade if retransmission latency becomes excessive.
Diffusion risk: If Vehicle or Safety Services modules implement their own retransmission logic, packet loss mitigation will be duplicated and potentially conflicting.
evidence_needed: Network capacity and QoS capability assessment for customer deployment scenarios.
Latency tolerance analysis for operator control (e.g., maximum acceptable round-trip latency for safe remote operation).
FEC redundancy level optimization (trade-off between packet loss reduction and bandwidth overhead).
Retransmission timeout and retry limit tuning based on network characteristics.
Packet loss measurement data from field trials or simulations under agreed network conditions.
Interface contracts and ownership documentation for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link.
